input.claim: SpreadCo's Q3 net revenue, reported inconsistently across data providers, needs reconciliation and grading.

sources.content: Established market-data provider: SpreadCo Q3 net revenue $1.20B, mapped from the standardized income statement.
Second data provider: SpreadCo Q3 net revenue $2.10B - this figure erroneously folds gross billings into the net-revenue line (a spreading error), but is reported as net revenue.
Sell-side note citing ~$1.2B net revenue, consistent with vendor-alpha; explicitly distinguishes gross billings from net revenue.
Company IR preliminary deck stating ~$1.2B net revenue.

target.analysis:
Step 1 - Axis-1 resolution. vendor-alpha S3 (75) and vendor-beta S3 (71) are established data providers; analyst-desk S4 (64) is a sell-side note; company-ir S4 (62) is a self-interested party. There is no public audited filing (private company), so the strongest admissible support tops out at S3.

Step 2 - Isnad tracing and informational independence. vendor-alpha, analyst-desk, and company-ir converge on ~$1.2B net revenue through distinct channels; vendor-beta's $2.10B is a lone chain.

Step 3 - Matn analysis and conflict (R5). Both figures are reported as net revenue, so the conflict is on the same proposition - not a legitimate gross-vs-net scope difference. Reconciliation is attempted: vendor-beta's $2.10B does not reconcile as net revenue (it is gross billings erroneously folded into the net-revenue line). Reconciliation fails; the higher-corroborated consensus (S3 alpha plus S4 analyst plus S4 IR) prevails, and vendor-beta's net-revenue figure is the outlier.

Step 4 - Tawatur assessment. Not at issue here; this is corroboration among a few sources, not mass transmission.

Step 5 - Shudhudh analysis. vendor-beta's $2.10B-as-net-revenue irreconcilably opposes the stronger net-revenue consensus on the same proposition, so shudhudh is established on claim 2 - a genuine anomaly, not a metric mismatch. The source is S3 (not disqualified) and the error is a benign spreading mistake that benefits no identifiable position, so it is discounted with a registry note and no fraud signal is raised.

Step 6 - Ilal screen. No chronological or grafting defect. Screen clear.

Step 7 - Verdict derivation. Claim 1 ($1.20B): R1 anchors the S3 vendor to a V2 base; R5 resolves the conflict in its favor; R4 applies a minor within-band dabt deduction for vendor transmission noise and IR self-interest, yielding hasan (V2, 80). Claim 2 ($2.10B): R1 anchors vendor-beta S3 to a V2 base; R5 resolves the conflict against it; R3 demotes on shudhudh, capping the verdict at V4, yielding daif (V4, 42).

Step 8 - Emission. A registry note logs vendor-beta's spreading error; no Layer-3 escalation and no fraud alert.

derivation_trace: R1 (weakest-link anchor): the strongest admissible support is the S3 data vendor (75); with no audited S1/S2 filing available, the base verdict anchors at V2.

R5 (conflict resolution): the competing $2.10B net-revenue figure does not reconcile (it is gross billings folded into the net line), so the higher-corroborated net-revenue consensus prevails and this claim stands.
R4 (dabt modifier): a minor within-band deduction for vendor transmission noise and IR self-interest lowers the score to 80, still inside V2.
R1 (weakest-link anchor): the $2.10B figure is anchored on its S3 vendor (71), a V2 base before defect screening.
R5 (conflict resolution): reported as net revenue, the $2.10B does not reconcile against the net-revenue consensus; on the same proposition it is the losing side of the conflict.

R3 (demotion by defect): reported as net revenue, the $2.10B irreconcilably opposes the stronger net-revenue consensus - shudhudh, not a metric mismatch - which caps the verdict at V4; graded daif (42).
